name: B.S. in Information Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most technically coherent of the informatics degrees: a programming arc (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> IT 114 Advanced Programming), a networking/systems arc (IT 120 -> IT 340 System Administration and IT 420 Computer Systems & Networks -> IT 490 Systems Integration), a data arc (IS 331 database), and design/apps (IT 201, IT 202) all converge on an IT 491 capstone. Term loads are steady at 13-16 credits and difficulty ramps sensibly. The only softness is the large block of eight generic 'Specialization Course' slots whose sequencing/content is not shown, so the concentration's internal progression is invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the named specialization tracks with their ordered course lists so the eight 'Specialization Course' slots read as a coherent concentration rather than interchangeable placeholders.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and slightly shallower than its IS siblings: deepestPrereqChain is 6 (IT 490 Systems Integration, gated on CS 113/115, IS 331/CS 331, and IT 340 -- a sensible terminal), with clear intermediate chains (IT 340/IT 420 on IT 120 or CS 356). missingCourseCount is 4 and all are peripheral -- GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and the one-credit YWCC 207/YWCC 307 seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (catalog YWCC 207/YWCC 307; re-tag GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories) so the otherwise clean IT prerequisite graph has no loose ends.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data. Given IT's ABET-style emphasis on applied competencies, the absence of a stated outcomes list is a meaningful catalog gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 learning outcomes covering programming, networking/system administration, database, systems integration, and professional practice, plus a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), and the required IT core is substantial and well-defined (IT 101/114/120/201/202/340/420/490 plus CS 100/113, IS 331/350). The transparency caveat is the large open block -- eight 'Specialization Courses' plus roughly seven 'General Electives' -- which is a lot of undefined credit even though the core anchors the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Show a credit-distribution summary and label the specialization block's credit total so the split between the defined IT core and the ~24 credits of specialization/free electives is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Cleaner than the IS B.A. -- fewer footnote artifacts -- but the same class of defects remains: GER rows are split with null credits ('Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 300+ level'), the COM alternative renders as a null-code 'or COM 312 or Oral Presentations' line, eight identical 'Specialization Course' placeholders carry no content, and YWCC titles are abbreviated ('Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Collapse the GER and 'or ...' rows into single readable lines and either name or link the eight specialization slots so the plan is not dominated by identical placeholders.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core covers durable IT skills -- programming (IT 114), networking (IT 120/IT 420), system administration (IT 340), internet applications (IT 202), systems integration (IT 490), and database (IS 331) -- but it reads as traditional on-premises IT. The single most conspicuous gap is the absence of any cybersecurity course despite a networking-and-sysadmin backbone that is the ideal foundation for it; there is also no cloud/DevOps, virtualization/containers, or infrastructure-automation content, all now central to IT roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required cybersecurity course (network/systems security) and a cloud-computing/DevOps course, so the networking + sysadmin base leads into the security and cloud skills employers now demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 19 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries (e.g., IT 340 system administration, IT 420 computer systems and networks, IT 490 systems integration, IS 331 database design). Content is informative and course-appropriate; only a few registrar titles are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and clean alternatives (IT 114: 'CS 113 or CS 115'; IT 202: 'CS 100 or CS 113 or CS 115 or a course in a high-level programming language as approved by department'; IT 490: 'CS 113 or CS 115, IS 331 or CS 331, and IT 340'), and intro courses correctly show none. The consistent, well-formed chains make this the clearest prerequisite set among the informatics degrees; minor phrasing variance keeps it from a 5.

missingTopics.rationale: A program built on networking (IT 120/IT 420) and system administration (IT 340) with no security course has a glaring gap; security is now inseparable from every IT operations role and should be a required course.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud computing and virtualization (AWS/Azure, containers, Kubernetes)
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340/IT 420/IT 490 are on-premises-centric; modern IT infrastructure is cloud and container based, so a cloud/virtualization course is essential currency.
IT 114 teaches application programming, but operational automation (infrastructure-as-code, pipelines) that defines contemporary IT work is absent.
Systems integration (IT 490) would benefit from ITSM practices and modern monitoring/observability that IT operations roles rely on daily.
Enterprise IT increasingly manages mobile and IoT endpoints, a natural extension of the networking/sysadmin core not currently represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The sysadmin/systems-integration roles this program trains for are shifting to cloud-native DevOps/SRE; IT 340/IT 490 should evolve toward automated, cloud-based operations.
Security is now central to every IT function; the program's networking + sysadmin base is the ideal launchpad for a zero-trust-oriented security track.
LLM-assisted troubleshooting and automated incident response are reshaping IT operations, a forward-looking addition to IT 490 systems integration.
Declarative, versioned infrastructure (Terraform/Ansible) is now standard practice and a direct modernization of the IT 340 system-administration content.
Distributed edge/IoT infrastructure extends the networking curriculum (IT 120/IT 420) into a fast-growing operational domain.

topSuggestions: Add a required cybersecurity course -- the largest gap given the networking and system-administration backbone.
Add cloud-computing/DevOps with virtualization/containers and infrastructure-as-code to modernize the on-prem-centric core.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview.
Name and sequence the eight 'Specialization Course' slots into published tracks so the concentration's progression is visible.
Clean presentation: collapse split GER and 'or ...' rows and replace identical specialization placeholders with real course listings.
